stem: At a certain time of day, the sun is at an angle of elevation of 53 degrees above the horizon. A vertical flagpole casts a shadow 24 feet long on flat ground. A student wants to find the straight-line distance from the tip of the shadow on the ground to the top of the flagpole. Given tan 53° = 4/3, what is this distance, in feet?
explanation: First, find the height of the flagpole using tan 53° = opposite/adjacent: height = 24 × (4/3) = 32 feet. The straight-line distance from the tip of the shadow to the top of the flagpole is the hypotenuse of the right triangle formed by the shadow (24 ft) and the flagpole (32 ft). Using the Pythagorean theorem: distance = √(24² + 32²) = √(576 + 1024) = √1600 = 40 feet.

distractorRationale.A: Computes the flagpole height correctly as 24 × (4/3) = 32 but reports this as the straight-line distance instead of applying the Pythagorean theorem = 32
distractorRationale.B: Inverts the tangent ratio, computing the flagpole height as 24 × (3/4) = 18, then applies the Pythagorean theorem: √(24² + 18²) = √(576 + 324) = √900 = 30
distractorRationale.C: Adds the shadow length and flagpole height instead of using the Pythagorean theorem: 24 + 32 = 56
distractorRationale.D: Correct: Computes flagpole height as 24 × (4/3) = 32, then finds the hypotenuse using the Pythagorean theorem: √(24² + 32²) = √1600 = 40

imageDescription: A right triangle diagram showing a vertical flagpole on the left, its shadow extending 24 feet along flat ground to the right, and a dashed line from the tip of the shadow to the top of the flagpole representing the sun ray. The angle of elevation at the tip of the shadow is labeled 53°. The shadow length (24 ft) is marked along the ground, the flagpole height is unlabeled, and the dashed hypotenuse (sun ray distance) is marked with a question mark.
